title: Beyond GDP: a better way to measure national prosperity?
gist: Economist Shunsuke Managi argues the UN-backed Inclusive Wealth Index should supplement GDP by pricing human, manufactured and natural capital, and uses AI plus satellite data to map mobility supply chains down to 500-metre resolution to expose hidden environmental and labor trade-offs.

insights.claim: GDP, the near-century-old global output measure, fails to adequately account for human wellbeing and the assets and costs of the natural environment, undermining its use for gauging resilience and sustainability.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Momentum toward broader prosperity metrics could reshape which national and corporate performance signals investors and regulators weight.
insights.quote: its limitations in measuring economic resilience and sustainability have long been recognized

insights.claim: The Inclusive Wealth Index prices human capital (knowledge, education, skills), manufactured capital (roads, buildings, machinery) and natural capital (forests, oceans, atmosphere, ecosystems) rather than only what is produced and sold.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: An asset-based accounting lens flags when growth erodes underlying capital, a screen for spotting unsustainable national or supply-chain models.

insights.claim: Determining the true cost of a supply chain requires accounting for its assets, not just outputs, revealing whether it creates lasting value or erodes the foundations it depends on.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Asset-erosion accounting could become the basis for future ESG and sustainability disclosure standards.
insights.quote: determining the true cost of a supply chain requires accounting for its assets, not just what it produces

insights.claim: Managi's team uses AI on company information, publicly available data and satellite imagery to map mobility supply-chain impacts across sectors, geography and time, down to a 500-metre grid resolution.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Grid-level supply-chain impact mapping is a concrete AI application that could underpin next-generation ESG data products.
insights.quote: down to a grid with a 500-metre resolution

insights.claim: The AI analysis assesses supply chains across three dimensions, well-being (including forced-labor risk), sustainability (beyond carbon to biodiversity and degradation) and resilience (withstanding geopolitical shifts and regulation).
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: A three-axis scoring scheme signals the metrics future supply-chain due diligence may standardize around.

insights.claim: Fine-grained mapping can expose trade-offs invisible to national averages, such as urban carbon reductions that cause environmental degradation or exploitative labor elsewhere in the supply chain.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Reveals greenwashing risk where headline emissions cuts merely displace harm, a red-flag detector for sustainability claims.
insights.quote: cases where reductions in urban carbon emissions lead to environmental degradation or exploitative labour practices elsewhere in the supply chain

insights.claim: A core obstacle is that no company truly understands its entire supply chain, which Managi frames as the central challenge in building measures of total economic output.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Corporate supply-chain opacity is the pain point AI-mapping tools aim to monetize.
insights.quote: No company truly understands its entire supply chain

insights.claim: Accounting for all supply-chain elements is especially hard in transport and mobility, where globalized inputs from battery raw-material extraction to infrastructure and energy make trade-offs difficult to track.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Mobility's tangled supply chains make it the proving ground where these AI accounting tools either work or fail.

insights.claim: The team is running ground projects in Bangkok, Thailand and Delhi, India studying infrastructure, traffic congestion and air pollution to move the Inclusive Wealth Index from theory into practice.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Real deployments in emerging-market megacities are the near-term proof points to watch for the metric's traction.
insights.quote: projects on the ground in Bangkok, Thailand and Delhi, India

insights.claim: The Inclusive Wealth Index was developed through UN-led research as part of its 'Beyond GDP' initiative, and Managi also directs an ESG-plus-AI tech startup, signaling commercialization of the approach.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: UN backing plus a founder-led startup marks where academic prosperity metrics are being turned into sellable ESG tooling.
insights.quote: director of a tech startup that combines environmental, social, and governance (ESG) perspective with AI
